label: ai
topic: Prisoners sentenced to eternal life are orbited around a planet in spacesuits
text:
Intake occurs without ceremony. The condemned are processed in groups of twelve, restrained and sedated, their identities reduced to serial designations. There is no formal acknowledgment of sentence. The crime, having already been adjudicated, is considered irrelevant to the procedure. The only relevant fact is that each subject has been assigned Continuance.

Continuance is administered in Facility Ring-9, a structure orbiting the prison planet at a fixed altitude of 3,200 kilometers. The facility is partitioned into sequential chambers: Preparation, Augmentation, Immobilization, Encapsulation, and Ejection. Each chamber operates continuously. Downtime is statistically negligible.

In Preparation, biological age is assessed and corrected. Subjects are stabilized to a median physiological state equivalent to early adulthood. Degenerative conditions are reversed. Injuries are repaired. Neural activity is monitored to ensure baseline cognitive function is preserved. Sedation is maintained at a level sufficient to prevent resistance but insufficient to induce unconsciousness.

In Augmentation, cellular decay is halted. Telomeric structures are reinforced. Regenerative processes are enhanced to a degree that exceeds natural limits. Subjects are rendered incapable of death by conventional means. This includes resistance to vacuum exposure, radiation, and extreme temperature variance. Pain receptors are not removed.

In Immobilization, motor function is permanently disabled. Neural pathways governing voluntary movement are severed or inhibited. Subjects retain full sensory awareness and cognitive capacity. They are capable of thought, memory, and perception, but not action. Vocalization is eliminated.

In Encapsulation, subjects are sealed within individual suits. The suits are constructed from composite materials designed to withstand micrometeoroid impact and long-term orbital degradation. Internal systems regulate minimal metabolic requirements. Visual interfaces are transparent. Subjects retain an unobstructed field of view.

In Ejection, subjects are transported via shuttle to the outer perimeter of the prison planet's ring system. The rings are composed not of particulate matter, but of prior subjects—billions of encapsulated bodies distributed across a stable orbital plane. New subjects are released at calculated intervals to maintain density and distribution.

Upon release, subjects join the existing formation. Orbital mechanics ensure perpetual motion. Relative positions shift gradually, creating patterns of clustering and dispersal. There is no collision avoidance system. Contact between subjects occurs at irregular intervals, producing minor impacts that are fully perceived.

Shuttle pilots are authorized to deviate from optimal flight paths during return trajectories. Informal practice has developed wherein pilots attempt to intersect with individual subjects at high velocity. Direct impact results in temporary displacement of the subject's orbit and structural stress to the suit. Fatality is not possible.

Pilot logs occasionally include subjective commentary. Common entries reference the visual effect of the rings: "dense," "luminous," "like a storm frozen in place." Some pilots assign informal identifiers to subjects they encounter repeatedly. These identifiers are not recognized by the system.

Over extended durations, subjects experience cumulative psychological effects. These include, but are not limited to, temporal distortion, memory recursion, and persistent awareness of proximity to other subjects. Communication is not possible. Recognition of other subjects is inferred through visual continuity.

There is no termination protocol. Continuance is indefinite.

Maintenance of the ring system is considered a success. The punishment is visible from the planet's surface as a bright band encircling the sky. It serves as both deterrent and symbol.

No further action is required.
